subject: UX & Research
title: Tim Berners-Lee's Accessibility Vision
description: A micro-lesson that makes Tim Berners-Lee's Accessibility Vision usable.

hook.text: The inventor of the web said: 'The power of the web is in its universality. Access by everyone.'

buildup.text: Tim Berners-Lee designed HTML to be device-independent. Text-based content that any machine could render.

discovery.text: HTML was inherently accessible. Headings, lists, and links worked with any reader. Then CSS and JS arrived.

twist.text: Rich web apps broke accessibility. Developers replaced native elements with custom ones that lack semantics.

climax.text: The W3C created WAI to restore the vision. Accessibility was designed in from the web's very beginning.

punchline.text: The web was built for everyone. We broke it. Now we're fixing it.

quiz.question: How was the original web inherently accessible?
options: HTML was text-based and device-independent, so any machine could render it
Early browsers had built-in screen readers
The first websites only used images with alt text
